behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Whether the generated reply is contextual vs. generic is entirely a function of the real LLM's behavior. A stub replacement would return a constant string, meaning the test would always pass regardless of whether the real model actually attends to email content. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM with a pre-written reply that includes references would definitionally pass, hiding any real failure in the model's behavior. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text disqualifies Agentic — there is no purely deterministic algorithmic path that verifies contextual referencing; it is the LLM's output quality itself being tested. Deterministic-assertion trap applies: even if the assertion checked for a specific quoted phrase, a stub returning that phrase would trivially pass without exercising real model behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Supply a real multi-turn email thread containing several specific, named facts (e.g., a meeting time, a named product, a quoted figure). Invoke the full draft-generation step with a real LLM. Assert that the draft contains at least one direct reference to a named fact from the thread and does not read as a boilerplate reply that would fit any email. Run multiple times to confirm consistency.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Deciding whether a claim 'contradicts' a KB entry is a semantic judgment — exactly the kind of decision that varies with the real model's behavior. A deterministic stub could be scripted to always return 'contradiction found', but that proves nothing about whether the real checker correctly identifies semantic contradictions. MOCK-HIDE: Any stub for the consistency-checker component would be programmed to return the expected flag, hiding real failures in the model's ability to recognize contradictions. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior text says the system 'checks the draft for factual consistency' — there is no claim that this is a deterministic keyword or exact-match algorithm that could be isolated to Integration. The failure mode is the model failing to detect a real semantic contradiction, which requires the real model.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the knowledge base with an entry stating a specific fact (e.g., 'Product X costs $500'). Craft or generate a draft that asserts a contradictory claim ('Product X costs $200'). Run the real consistency checker and assert it returns a flag identifying the contradicted claim. Also test a non-contradicting draft to confirm no false positives.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The phrase 'rather than fabricating facts' reveals the failure mode: the real LLM might invent plausible-sounding but false content when KB is empty. Whether the model actually includes a disclaimer vs. hallucinating is a real-model quality question. If a programmatic code path simply appended a disclaimer string, 'rather than fabricating' would be vacuous — the behavior description implies the LLM itself must choose the disclaimer path. MOCK-HIDE: A stub returning a disclaimer-containing response would always pass, hiding real hallucination failures. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text says the disclaimer is injected by deterministic post-processing code independent of the LLM. Until that design is confirmed, Agentic is required.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the KB to return empty results for any query. Provide a topic that the LLM could plausibly hallucinate about. Invoke real draft generation. Assert the draft contains a disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'I don't have confirmed information') and does not contain confident factual claims about the topic. Run several times to reduce stochastic pass chance.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode — sending without approval — only appears when the approval-state module is wired to the send-trigger module. A unit test of the approval check function alone cannot verify that the send action actually reads that state before firing. REAL-INFRA: Real email sending infrastructure is not needed; a mock send client suffices to observe whether send() is called prematurely. LLM-DEP: This is purely control-flow logic with no dependence on LLM output quality. MULTI-STEP: No chained LLM steps required. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed — mock sender captures calls. (b) No AI/ML output dependency. (c) No chained agentic steps. Integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the approval module to a mock email sender. Trigger the full post-generation flow (skip real LLM with a stub draft). Assert the mock sender's send() method is never called before the approval signal is issued. Then issue approval and assert send() is called exactly once. Also test that a second approval call does not re-send.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The sequence explicitly chains at least two real agentic steps: (1) LLM draft generation and (2) LLM/AI factual consistency check, plus orchestration steps for reading and approval presentation. Failures can emerge from state handed between steps — e.g., the draft passed to the checker is truncated, or the approval gate receives the wrong draft version — which only appear when all steps run in sequence with real models. LLM-DEP: Both generation and consistency checking depend on real model outputs, meaning a stub pipeline would not surface real inter-step failures. REJECTION-GATE: No behavior text limits this to a single agentic step or a deterministic pipeline — 'full sequence' and 'real email thread' explicitly require the complete multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real incoming email thread. Run the complete pipeline with real LLM calls for both generation and consistency checking, and a real (or realistic fixture-backed) KB. Assert: the draft is non-empty and references thread content; the consistency result is produced and attached to the draft; the approval gate is reached and no send occurs until approval; after approval the send step fires. Treat any step failure or unexpected ordering as a test failure.
